topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation to Dr. Vance’s office felt like a physical blow. Liam smoothed down his already immaculate shirt, the fabric suddenly feeling too tight across his chest. He’d been dragged there by his sister, a forced social obligation that now felt like a public execution. He hadn't expected to see Mark, the boy who used to dismantle his lunch money with casual cruelty in the seventh grade, now sitting behind a mahogany desk, radiating a calm authority that was utterly foreign. When Mark looked up, his eyes—those same sharp, assessing eyes—met Liam’s. There was no sneer, no lingering malice, just a gentle, almost clinical observation. Liam mumbled a greeting, his voice cracking slightly, and immediately focused on the pattern in the expensive rug beneath his polished shoes, desperate to become invisible.

[story 2]

"So, you're the one who...?" Sarah asked, her voice a mixture of awe and thinly veiled awkwardness. I gripped the strap of my handbag so tightly my knuckles were white. It was Mark. The architect of my most awkward middle school years, the one who perfected the art of making me feel small. Now, he was wearing a soft, dove-grey sweater, and his smile was practiced, warm, and utterly disarming. Every time he leaned forward to listen to my rambling anxieties, a hot flush crept up my neck, making my cheeks feel strangely prickly. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, acutely aware of how clumsy my movements were, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The consultation room smelled faintly of sandalwood and old paper, a scent that somehow managed to feel both comforting and intensely exposing. I tried to occupy myself by focusing on the framed prints on the wall, but my gaze kept snagging on Mark. He was discussing cognitive reframing techniques with a nervous patient, his hands gesturing with precise, measured movements. When he turned his attention to me, a sudden, sharp awareness of my own ill-fitting clothes and the tremor in my hands made me want to retreat. I cleared my throat too loudly, the sound echoing in the quiet room. I felt a desperate need to prove that I was capable of more than just stammering, but all I could manage was a tight, shallow breath.
